question: How did Linus Torvalds respond to Christoph Hellwig's claim that Rust code had been merged into the Linux kernel over his objections as DMA maintainer?
must_contain: Torvalds's February 2025 rebuttal: the disputed pull request did not touch the DMA layer at all — it merely used the DMA API from a separate subdirectory — and a maintainer does not get to control where his subsystem is used.
facts: The dispute was over Rust bindings calling the kernel's DMA API, February 2025
Torvalds wrote the pull request 'DID NOT TOUCH THE DMA LAYER AT ALL'
Torvalds rejected the position 'as a DMA maintainer I control what the DMA code is used for' as 'not how any of this works'
hardness_note: One search describes the drama's existence but not the content of Torvalds's rebuttal.
answer:
I have all the information I need. The Phoronix article quotes Torvalds' full email, and the Slashdot article corroborates the key points.

Here is the detailed account of Linus Torvalds' response to Christoph Hellwig's claim that Rust code had been merged into the Linux kernel over his objections as DMA maintainer.

---

## Linus Torvalds' Response to Christoph Hellwig

On **February 20, 2025**, Linus Torvalds posted a lengthy email to the Linux kernel mailing list (LKML) responding directly to Christoph Hellwig, who had objected to Rust code being merged and had claimed that Torvalds said in private he would merge Rust code over a maintainer's objections. Torvalds' response was unusually direct and forceful. Here is what he said, point by point.

### 1. The pull request did not touch the DMA layer at all

Torvalds opened by correcting a factual error in Hellwig's objection:

> "The fact is, the pull request you objected to DID NOT TOUCH THE DMA LAYER AT ALL. It was literally just another user of it, in a completely separate subdirectory, that didn't change the code you maintain in _any_ way, shape, or form."

He called Hellwig's repeated complaints about this "complete garbage arguments."

### 2. A maintainer does not control who uses their code

Torvalds directly refuted Hellwig's premise that being the DMA maintainer gave him a veto over new consumers of the DMA APIs:

> "Honestly, what you have been doing is basically saying 'as a DMA maintainer I control what the DMA code is used for.' And that is not how *any* of this works."

He drove the point home with an analogy:

> "What's next? Saying that particular drivers can't do DMA, because you don't like that device, and as a DMA maintainer you control who can use the DMA code? That's _literally_ exactly what you are trying to do with the Rust code."

### 3. "You can't have it both ways"

Torvalds directly addressed the contradiction in Hellwig's position:

> "You can't say 'I want to have nothing to do with Rust', and then in the very next sentence say 'And that means that the Rust code that I will ignore cannot use the C interfaces I maintain'."

He was explicit about the consequence: "If you don't want to deal with the Rust code, you get no *say* on the Rust code."

### 4. The "wall of protection" goes both ways

Torvalds explained that the promise that C maintainers don't have to deal with Rust code also means they don't get a say in Rust code:

> "Put another way: the 'nobody is forced to deal with Rust' does not imply 'everybody is allowed to veto any Rust code'."

He noted that maintainers who *want* to be involved with the Rust side "will have some say in what the Rust bindings look like" — they "basically become the maintainers of the Rust interfaces too." But those who take the "I don't want to deal with Rust" option "also then basically will obviously not have to bother with the Rust bindings — but as a result they also won't have any say on what goes on on the Rust side."

### 5. Personal respect, but calling out "your bullshit"

Despite the harsh substance, Torvalds framed it as a disagreement between peers. He wrote:

> "I respect you technically, and I like working with you. And no, I am not looking for yes-men, and I like it when you call me out on my bullshit. I say some stupid things at times, there needs to be people who just stand up to me and tell me I'm full of shit. But now I'm calling you out on *YOURS*."

### 6. The broader principle

Torvalds framed the issue as not being about Rust policy at all, but about a fundamental question of maintainer authority:

> "So this email is not about some 'Rust policy'. This email is about a much bigger issue: as a maintainer you are in charge of your code, sure — but you are not in charge of who uses the end result and how."

### Aftermath

Days after Torvalds' email, on **February 26, 2025**, Christoph Hellwig stepped down as maintainer of the DMA-mapping tree and the DMA-mapping subsystem. Marek Szyprowski took over as the maintainer, with the change recorded in the MAINTAINERS file (commit `f7d5db965f3e`). Hellwig continues to work on other areas of the kernel, but no longer maintains DMA mapping.

**Sources:**
- [Phoronix: Linus Torvalds Clearly Lays Out Linux Maintainer Roles - Or Not - Around Rust Code](https://www.phoronix.com/news/Torvalds-On-Rust-Maintainers) — Contains the full text of Torvalds' email.
- [Slashdot: Torvalds: Rust Kernel Code Isn't Forced In Over Maintainers' Objections](https://linux.slashdot.org/story/25/02/22/0524210/torvalds-rust-kernel-code-isnt-forced-in-over-maintainers-objections) — Summarizes the key exchanges and quotes.

question: According to their most recent 10-K filings, how many more employees does Microsoft have than Nvidia?
must_contain: Roughly 186,000 more (Microsoft ~228,000 in its FY2025 10-K vs Nvidia ~42,000 in its FY2026 10-K).
facts: Microsoft reported ~228,000 full-time employees as of June 30, 2025
Nvidia reported ~42,000 employees in its FY2026 10-K (fiscal year ended January 2026)
hardness_note: Snippets offer stale or conflicting headcounts (36k Nvidia, 241.9k Microsoft from non-filing sources); the filing numbers plus arithmetic are needed.

question: What is the population of Nigeria, and why is the number contested?
must_contain: Official/UN-derived estimates put it around 230-240 million, but the figure is contested: Nigeria hasn't held a census since 2006, its own National Population Commission admits it lacks accurate figures, and credible critics argue the true number may be tens of millions lower.
facts: UN-based estimates are ~240 million (2026); the last census was 2006
The National Population Commission has publicly admitted it lacks accurate population figures
Skeptics estimate the real figure could be 170-180 million; a biometric census has been planned
hardness_note: One search returns a single estimate; the institutional story (census gap, NPC admission, overcounting incentives) requires more.
answer:
## Population of Nigeria

As of 2026, Nigeria's population is estimated at **242,431,832**, according to the United Nations World Population Prospects (2024 Revision), as reported by Worldometer [Nigeria Population - Worldometer](https://www.worldometers.info/world-population/nigeria-population). World Population Review provides the same figure of 242,431,832 as of July 2, 2026 [Nigeria Population 2026 - World Population Review](https://worldpopulationreview.com/countries/nigeria). This makes Nigeria the **6th most populous country in the world** and the **most populous in Africa**, representing approximately 3.03% of the global population and 15.3% of Africa's population [Nigeria Population 2026 - World Population Review](https://worldpopulationreview.com/countries/nigeria).

Nigeria's population growth rate is about **2.1% per year**, with a net increase of roughly 13,493 people per day (21,114 births, 7,613 deaths, and net emigration of 8 people). The median age is 18.3 years, and the population density is 266 people per km² (689 per mi²) [Nigeria Population - Worldometer](https://www.worldometers.info/world-population/nigeria-population).

Projections indicate Nigeria's population will continue growing rapidly: estimated at 262 million by 2030, 359 million by 2050, and potentially 476 million by 2097 [Nigeria Population - Worldometer](https://www.worldometers.info/world-population/nigeria-population); [Nigeria Population 2026 - World Population Review](https://worldpopulationreview.com/countries/nigeria).

---

## Why the Number Is Contested

Nigeria's population figures have been deeply contested for decades. Several interconnected factors drive the controversy:

### 1. A History of Discredited Censuses

Every population census in Nigeria since independence has sparked major disputes. The first post-independence census in **1962** was withdrawn after allegations of rigging. The **1963** census was also widely questioned. The **1973** census was completely discredited and its results never officially published. No census was conducted in **1981**. The **1991** census was marred by allegations of cheating — for instance, in some southern states, every household form reported exactly nine members (the maximum that fit on the form), a statistically impossible pattern. The **2006** census — the most recent actual enumeration — produced a total of 140 million, but was immediately rejected by many southern leaders [PRB - Objections Surface Over Nigerian Census Results](https://www.prb.org/resource/objections-surface-over-nigerian-census-results).

### 2. No Census Since 2006

Nigeria has not conducted a national census in **20 years** — the last one was in 2006. A new census was planned for 2023 but was postponed twice, first due to the presidential election and then due to the government transition program. As of 2026, the next census has not yet been held [CGTN Africa - Postponement of Nigerian population census](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yt3RHhlNULQ); [Premium Times - Real reasons census was postponed](https://www.premiumtimesng.com/news/top-news/595976-real-reasons-census-was-postponed-official.html). This means all current population figures are projections based on the disputed 2006 baseline, not fresh counts.

### 3. North-South Rivalry and Political Power

Census numbers directly affect political power in Nigeria. Under the constitution, the distribution of seats in the House of Representatives and the "federal character" principle (which dictates that government appointments reflect the relative strengths of ethnic and religious groups) both depend on population data. Historically, northern states have been recorded as having a larger combined population than the south, which has given the north control of the federal government since independence. Southern leaders have long argued that this is demographically implausible, given the north's semi-arid climate compared to the more densely populated coastal south. As the BBC noted, "the census dictates who really holds power" [BBC - Nigeria's counting controversy](http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/africa/4512240.stm).

### 4. Resource Allocation (The "Revenue Formula")

Federal revenue — about 90% of which comes from oil produced in the south — is distributed to Nigeria's 36 states based partly on population size. The larger a state's population, the larger its share of federal funds. This creates a powerful incentive for state governors to inflate their numbers. During the 2006 census, Lagos State Governor Bola Tinubu claimed that a parallel census conducted by Lagos in collaboration with the National Population Commission showed the state had **more than 17.5 million** residents, not the 9.0 million the official census recorded [PRB - Objections Surface Over Nigerian Census Results](https://www.prb.org/resource/objections-surface-over-nigerian-census-results). The difference in revenue from being counted at 17.5 million versus 9.0 million is enormous.

### 5. Ethnic and Religious Tensions

Census figures also determine the perceived numerical strength of Nigeria's major ethnic groups (Hausa, Yoruba, Igbo) and religions (Islam at about 53.5%, Christianity at about 45.9%). Muslim northern leaders have historically opposed including questions about religion and ethnicity in census forms, while Christian southern leaders have demanded them. The debate over whether Nigeria has more Muslims or Christians is one of the most sensitive political questions in the country. The BBC reported that in 2005-2006, northern leaders threatened to boycott any census that included questions on religion, while southeastern governors threatened to boycott any census that did not [BBC - Nigeria's counting controversy](http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/africa/4512240.stm).

### 6. Possible Overcount of 50 Million People

Some independent researchers argue that Nigeria's official population figures may be significantly inflated — by as much as **50 million people**. According to an analysis published on the Effective Altruism Forum, Nigeria's actual population could be closer to **170-180 million** rather than the widely cited 220-240 million. The evidence includes: satellite imagery and GIS analysis suggesting the 2006 census overcount in urban areas; National ID enrollment reaching only about 104 million by end of 2023 (though registration rates vary widely by state, from 73% in Lagos to 28% in Bayelsa); and the fact that a SIM-NIN verification exercise caused the loss of 64.3 million mobile subscriptions, suggesting that many "people" on the rolls may not exist [Effective Altruism Forum - Nigeria's Missing 50 Million People](https://forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/824rsHCXuqTmBb8se/nigeria-s-missing-50-million-people).

### 7. Technical and Logistical Challenges

Conducting a census in Nigeria is extraordinarily difficult. During the 2006 census, thousands of enumerators walked off the job because they hadn't been paid. Some areas are only accessible by water, but enumerators were reluctant to use poorly built boats and most Nigerians cannot swim. Rough terrain, poor roads, and lack of reliable transport further hamper enumeration [PRB - Objections Surface Over Nigerian Census Results](https://www.prb.org/resource/objections-surface-over-nigerian-census-results).

### 8. Political Distrust

Deep-seated mutual suspicion means that even if a census were technically perfect, it would likely still be rejected by whichever region feels disadvantaged. As Holly Newby, a former U.S. survey manager in Nigeria, put it: "The problem with Nigeria and doing any data collection effort is it could be the god's honest truth. You could have the most exact census, and there will always be people who don't believe it" [PRB - Objections Surface Over Nigerian Census Results](https://www.prb.org/resource/objections-surface-over-nigerian-census-results).

---

**In summary**, Nigeria's population as of 2026 is estimated at approximately **242 million**, but this number is contested because every census since independence has been marred by allegations of manipulation, the last actual census was in 2006, and the stakes — political power, federal revenue, and ethnic/religious status — are so high that all population figures are viewed with profound skepticism by large segments of the population. Some independent analysts believe the true number could be 50 million lower than official projections.
